# Command {#sec-command}

::: {.pattern-category}
Behavioural · Pattern 10 of 14
:::

::: {.gof-box}
Encapsulate a request as an object, thereby letting you parameterize clients with different requests, queue or log requests, and support undoable operations.

::: {.gof-source}
@gamma1994design, p. 233
:::
:::

## The Translation Argument

The Command pattern encapsulates a request as a self-contained object. That object carries everything needed to execute the request — its parameters, its receiver, its context — and can be stored, queued, logged, transmitted, and inspected independently of the moment it executes. The four properties GoF identifies — parameterisation, queuing, logging, and undo — all translate meaningfully to agentic AI. One of them translates more powerfully than GoF anticipated.

**Parameterisation.** Tool invocations as Command objects. Each carries the tool name, inputs, agent context, and active schema version. The command can be inspected, validated, and routed before execution. The `ToolRegistryFacade` executes commands; it does not accept raw function calls.

**Queuing.** Commands can be deferred, batched, and prioritised. The `RateLimitDecorator` is a command queue upstream of execution. Parallel tool calls are queued to respect concurrency limits. Urgent commands can be promoted without changing the invoker.

**Logging as data product.** This is the strongest translation. EDP1 — the Agent Interaction Trace — is a Command log. Every tool invocation, every reasoning step, every agent interaction is a Command object with full provenance. This is not a log entry. It is a governed data asset — versioned, schematised, and queryable across runs. The entire eval-as-data-product infrastructure rests on Command as the atomic unit of provenance.

**Rollback, not undo.** GoF undo reverses a single operation deterministically. Agentic systems cannot replay commands deterministically — the same inputs produce different LLM outputs. What is possible is rollback: the command's `mementoRef` field links to the state snapshot taken before it executed. When rollback is needed, the system restores that Memento snapshot. Command identifies what went wrong; Memento provides the restoration point. Together they form the agentic equivalent of undo.

The four GoF roles translate as follows:

| GoF Role | Agentic Equivalent | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| Command | `AgentCommand` interface | Defines `execute()`, `undo()`, and `getProvenance()`. All concrete commands implement it. |
| Concrete Command | `ToolInvocationCommand`, `DataAccessCommand`, `MacroCommand` | Encapsulates a specific request with all parameters. Carries full provenance. Storable, queueable, replayable. |
| Invoker | `AgentRunner`, `ToolRegistryFacade` | Holds a command and calls `execute()`. Manages the command queue and command log. Does not know what the command does internally. |
| Receiver | External tool, data source, model | The actual resource the command interacts with. Unaware of the Command wrapper. |

: GoF roles translated to the agentic provenance and rollback context {#tbl-command-roles}

The `MacroCommand` deserves explicit treatment. A `PlanExecutionCommand` wraps a full multi-step workflow — plan, execute, verify — as a single command. It delegates to individual `ToolInvocationCommands` internally. From the outside it is one command with one undo entry. This enables workflow-level rollback rather than step-level rollback.

## Command as Governed Data Asset {#sec-command-edp}

Every `ToolInvocationCommand` carries the following provenance fields as first-class structured data:

- `agentId` — which agent issued the command
- `toolName` — which tool was called
- `inputs` — full input parameters
- `output` — full result returned
- `timestamp` — execution time
- `schemaVersion` — `ToolSchemaCatalog` version active at execution
- `decoratorStack` — which wrappers were active
- `sessionId` — which session this belongs to
- `mementoRef` — link to the state snapshot before execution
- `evalVerdict` — EDP2 judge score, attached post-execution

This structure makes the Command Log EDP1 — the Agent Interaction Trace. EDP2 judge scores from the Abstract Factory are attached to Command records after execution. EDP3 aggregates over the Command Log. EDP4 is a curated subset labelled as ground truth. The eval data product infrastructure rests on Command as its atomic unit.

Critically, the architecture produces evaluation outputs not as transient signals but as governed data assets — versioned, schematised, and queryable across runs. Eval-as-data-product without Command is a goal without infrastructure.

## The Stochasticity Delta {#sec-command-delta}

::: {.callout-warning .callout-delta}
## Stochasticity Delta

**Commands are not idempotent.** In GoF, undo restores the prior state deterministically — the same object state, every time. In agentic AI, replaying a command — calling the same tool with the same inputs — may produce a different result. True undo in the GoF sense is not possible for LLM-based commands. What is possible is rollback: the command's `mementoRef` field links to the Memento snapshot taken before execution. When rollback is needed, the system restores that snapshot rather than reversing the command's effects. Command identifies the problem; Memento provides the restoration point. The two patterns compose into the agentic equivalent of undo, but the mechanism is fundamentally different from GoF's.

**Pre-execution validation may be stochastic.** Before a `ToolInvocationCommand` executes, the `GuardrailDecorator` validates it against the `SafetyConstraintStore`. If validation involves an LLM safety judge — a `SafetyJudge` from the Abstract Factory evaluating whether the command's intent is safe — that validation is probabilistic. The same command might be approved on one run and blocked on another. This pre-execution stochastic validation layer has no GoF equivalent.
:::

## Structural Diagram

The minimal diagram (@fig-command-minimal) shows the Invoker, the `AgentCommand` interface, the three concrete command types, the Command Log as EDP1 governed data asset, and the stochasticity delta.

![Command pattern — structural diagram. Invoker, AgentCommand interface, ToolInvocationCommand, DataAccessCommand, and MacroCommand feeding the Command Log as EDP1 governed data asset. Non-idempotency and stochastic pre-execution validation annotated in delta.](../../figures/command-minimal.svg){#fig-command-minimal}

## Canonical Example — NLI Tool Invocations and Eval Data Pipeline

In NLI, every tool invocation and every data access call is encapsulated as a Command before execution. Neither the `ToolRegistryFacade` nor the `DataProductAccessAgent` accepts raw function calls.

When the `PersonalDataQAAgent` retrieves an HRV recovery data product, it constructs a `DataAccessCommand` with the product ID, its own agent ID, the current consent reference, and the active schema version. The `DataProductAccessAgent` Facade executes it — validating consent, recording lineage, enforcing access, logging the audit entry. The command is appended to the Command Log as an EDP1 record. The `TaskAgentEvalFactory`'s `OutputJudge` attaches an EDP2 verdict post-execution.

When the `PlannerCoachAgent` constructs a study plan, its entire workflow is encapsulated as a `PlanExecutionCommand` — a `MacroCommand` containing retrieval, synthesis, and formatting sub-commands. If the student flags the plan as unhelpful, the system retrieves the `mementoRef` linking to the pipeline state before that command executed, restores the Memento snapshot, and re-runs the planning workflow with adjusted parameters.

The accumulated Command Log is the substrate for all longitudinal analysis in NLI. The `PatternWatcherAgent`'s Observer relationship with the Command Log enables it to detect patterns across a student's interaction history — which tools are invoked, at what frequency, with what success rates — without reprocessing raw data.

## Composability {#sec-command-composability}

**Memento** is Command's closest structural partner. Command records what happened — complete provenance of every action. Memento records the state when it happened — a snapshot of the pipeline. Together they provide agentic rollback. A Command Log without Memento tells you what went wrong but cannot restore prior state. Memento without Command tells you where the system was but not what caused the deviation.

**Abstract Factory** connects through the eval pipeline. The judge suite attaches EDP2 scores to Command records after execution. EDP1 is produced by Command; EDP2 is attached by Abstract Factory; EDP3 and EDP4 derive from the accumulated Command-plus-verdict records.

**Facade** is the execution point. The `ToolRegistryFacade` executes `ToolInvocationCommands`. The `DataProductAccessAgent` executes `DataAccessCommands`. Commands carry the request; the Facade carries it out. This separation keeps commands as pure provenance objects independent of execution infrastructure.

**Decorator** wraps the Facade. The `GuardrailDecorator` validates commands before execution. The `LoggingDecorator` ensures every executed command is captured as an EDP1 record.

**Observer** watches the Command Log. The `PatternWatcherAgent` is notified when new Command records are appended. The EDP3 aggregation pipeline is notified when new judge verdicts are attached.

**Chain of Responsibility** processes commands before execution. Every command flowing through `nli_api` passes through authentication, validation, rate limiting, and consent handlers before reaching the Facade.

::: {.composability-tags}
<span class="ctag"><strong>Memento</strong> — rollback infrastructure</span>
<span class="ctag"><strong>Abstract Factory</strong> — EDP2 verdicts attached to records</span>
<span class="ctag"><strong>Facade</strong> — command execution point</span>
<span class="ctag"><strong>Decorator</strong> — validation and logging wrappers</span>
<span class="ctag"><strong>Observer</strong> — watches Command Log for patterns</span>
<span class="ctag"><strong>Chain of Responsibility</strong> — pre-execution validation chain</span>
:::
